basic_info.title: Embedded Generation Investment Programme (“EGIP”)
basic_info.description:
Background  
South Africa is a contributor to global climate change, with greenhouse gas (“GHG”) emissions resulting mainly from energy production and consumption. The energy sector remains the single largest contributor to the country’s total GHG emissions (82.7% in 2012) in South Africa (the 2000 -2015 GHG inventory report is currently in development). The country is heavily reliant on coal-based fossil fuel energy, with more than 90% of electricity being produced mainly from this source. This dependence on coal continues to strain the country’s efforts to reduce GHG emissions. In 2012 the total GHG emissions in South Africa were estimated at 518,297 CO2 eq (including FOLU), of which 428,112 CO2 eq was contributed by the energy industry1. As a signatory to the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change (“UNFCCC”) and the Paris Agreement, South Africa has taken steps to formulate measures to mitigate and adapt to a changing climate. In the National Determined Contributions (“NDC”) submitted in 2015, South Africa has committed to a peak, plateau and decline in GHG emissions trajectory in the range between 398 and 614 Mt CO2eq for the period 2025 up to 2030. In line with South Africa’s commitments to reduce emissions, the promulgated Integrated Resource Plan 2010–2030 imposed CO2 emission limits on the electricity generation plan. South Africa’s power sector has to comply with CO2 emissions constraints: Peak-plateau-decline and a determined carbon budget for the entire sector up to 2050.This Programme along with the existing ongoing sovereign-backed Renewable Energy Independent Power Producers Procurement Programme (“REIPPPP”) will contribute towards these NDC targets.  In line with the national commitment to transition to a low carbon economy, the Department of Energy (“DoE”) designed the sovereign-backed REIPPPP. Since its inception in 2011, the REIPPPP has procured renewable energy capacity of ca. 6,422 MW (ca. 36% of renewable energy target) from 92 large independent power producer (“IPPs”) and 99 MW from 20 small scale (1-5 MW) IPPs.2 Up to 2015, South Africa had the fastest growing green economy in the world and the program was lauded as one of the most successful renewable energy programs in the world. The REIPPPP has stalled since the end of 2015 due to liquidity issues at the national utility (Eskom). The PPAs for the 27 large REIPPPP projects from rounds 3.5 and 4 which were affected by the delays and were on hold since 2015, were signed in April 2018.   Draft Integrated Resource Plan (“IRP”) 2018 Recognising the limitation of relying only on government backed renewable energy programmes, the Minister of Energy has published the updated draft IRP 2018 in August 2018 for public comment. The updated IRP indicates a total shift in country’s energy mix towards renewable energy, where it is anticipated that PV will represent 10.5% (7,958 MW) of energy by 2030 and Wind will represent 15.1% (11,442 MW). The draft IRP also allocates 200 MW per annum starting in 2018 for embedded generation capacity (generation for own use allocation) up to 2030 (2,600 MW allocated up to 2030). Embedded generation is the production of electricity from smaller scale power stations, located in close proximity to the place of consumption that are usually directly connected to a distribution network. In South Africa, these includes projects that lie outside of the formal government procurement programmes and usually defined as projects that are planned for own use/consumption.   Rationale for the Programme The country has a number of private sector backed fully-developed renewable energy projects, targeted at generation for own use. Some projects were developed for REIPPPP but were not successfully awarded preferred bidders. Out of a total of bankable projects, only 24% were successful in being awarded under the REIPPPP. There remains ca 11.2 GW of fully developed and viable renewable energy projects with necessary permitting and licensing in the country that can potentially be supported through the proposed Embedded Generation Investment Programme (“EGIP”). Embedded generation renewable energy projects have no demonstrable track record of successfully reaching financial close. The key objective of the EGIP is to formulate this track record to ensure that the initial Projects reach financial close, by so doing, a market for embedded generation is created in South Africa.  In order to improve the viability and the bankability of these Projects outside of the REIPPPP, the DBSA has structured a credit support mechanism to support non-sovereign backed PPAs for renewable energy projects in South Africa. Nonsovereign backed renewable energy projects are defined as projects that are wholly implemented by: (i) Private sector entities (acting as off-takers and Independent Power Producers (“IPPs”); and (ii) Local government entities (acting as off-takers) and private sector entities (acting as IPPs). This includes both the private and municipal off-takers. The embedded generation allocation per annum will further strengthen this pipeline. The objective is to credit enhance the projects under the Programme, thereby creating an enabling environment for continued renewable energy investment by the private sector in South Africa. In addition, this financing mechanism is intended to crowd-in funding from commercial lenders and to assist South Africa to make further inroads towards their climate change objectives. The intention from the South African Government (as articulated under the draft IRP) is that embedded generation contributes approximately 11.5% to renewable energy capacity by 2030.  The expected direct CO2 emissions reduction by the new renewable energy generation are ca. 717,794 tons CO2 eq per year once all the sub-projects are operational.

risk.description: Repayment/ Default Risk by the Off-taker (s)
risk.mitigation: The credibility and credit worthiness of the off-taker(s) to be assessed in detail during due diligence stage. PPA direct agreements are to be concluded in order to afford lenders step in rights in the eventuality of a default. The security package to be negotiated is to be robust, typical of project finance projects of a similar nature. In addition, the financing plan will include a debt service reserve account of no less than 6 months of project debt service, which will be used to pay debt in the event of any cash shortfall from the Borrower.

risk.description: Regulatory
risk.mitigation: This risk is mitigated by the South Africa’s strong regulatory framework that enables the implementation of renewable energy. NERSA is strongly supportive of the entrance of the private sector in the electricity market. The Programme will provide valuable insights into any potential weaknesses of the regulatory framework to further strengthen the development of the renewable energy sector in South Africa.

risk.description: Foreign exchange risk
risk.mitigation:
1) Hedging: DBSA shall enter into appropriate hedging arrangements, to cover the entire tenor of the relevant Programme Loan, including currency swapping arrangements with commercial banks on behalf of the Sub-Borrowers. The hedging arrangements will ensure that any ZAR amounts received from the relevant Sub-Borrower for the GCF-funded portion of the Programme Loans, on the respective due dates, are converted back to USD pursuant to such hedging arrangements, such that payment obligations of DBSA to GCF in USD are fully met. DBSA shall ensure that the Sub-Borrowers are contractually obligated to cover any insufficiency in the amounts (in USD) owed to GCF, in the event that the hedging arrangements for any reason are inadequate.
2) Conversion to ZAR: The respective USD amount of the GCF Proceeds in relation to the relevant amounts committed by DBSA under a Programme Loan shall be converted into ZAR (South Africa Rand) at the prevailing exchange spot rate available at the Standard Bank of South Africa (or, if not available there, at other local commercial banks acceptable to DBSA), under a hedging mechanism to be arranged by DBSA along appropriate documentation, including but not limited to International Swaps and Derivatives Association Agreements, collectively referred to as the “Hedging Mechanism”. DBSA will then transfer and hold such converted amount of GCF Proceeds in the - ZAR-Loan Account (as defined in the term sheet) until such proceeds are on-lent and disbursed to the Sub-Borrower in accordance with the relevant Programme Loan

risk.description: Foreign exchange risk
risk.mitigation: Conversion to ZAR: The respective USD amount of the GCF Proceeds in relation to the relevant amounts committed by DBSA under a Programme Loan    shall be converted into ZAR (South Africa Rand) at the prevailing exchange spot rate available at the Standard Bank of South Africa (or, if not available there, at other local commercial banks acceptable to DBSA), under a hedging mechanism to be arranged by DBSA along appropriate documentation, including but not limited to International Swaps and Derivatives Association Agreements, collectively referred to as the “Hedging Mechanism”. DBSA will then transfer and hold such converted amount of GCF Proceeds in the - ZAR-Loan Account (as defined in the term sheet) until such proceeds are on-lent and disbursed to the Sub-Borrower in accordance with the relevant Programme Loan.

risk.description: Energy resource risk
risk.mitigation: Lower wind availability or solar irradiation presents a risk for the Project due to its uncertainty and potential impact on the capacity of the Project to generate revenues. The energy resources will be independently verified by the Lenders Technical Advisor(s) to confirm P90 and P50 energy yields. According to the IPP quarterly report June 2017, 44 projects, which have been operational for longer than one year, generated energy equalling 90% of their P50 projections.  Important to note that the DBSA uses the more conservative P90 prediction to assess the renewable energy projects. 24 of the 44 projects have exceeded the P50 projections.  This mitigates to a certain extent the energy resource risk in the country.

risk.description: Completion, Operational and technology risk
risk.mitigation: The completion risk is to be mitigated for each project through the use of fixed price turnkey EPC Contract including appropriate contingencies and liquidated damages. For operations, suitable qualified O&M contactors with a solid track record, technical expertise and financial strength will be selected for each project.  Lenders Technical Advisors will also be required to opine and sign off on the said characteristics. The Programme will only utilise commercially proven technologies that have a solid track record in the market.

outcomes.name: M1.0 Reduced emissions through increased low-emission energy access and power generation

kpis.name: M.1.1 Tonnes of carbon dioxide equivalent (t CO2eq) reduced or avoided from  energy access and power generation
kpis.means_of_verification: Programme monitoring reports
kpis.assumptions: The DBSA uses a harmonized approach for assessing the mitigation benefits, or net greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions, of EGIP in accordance with the International Financial Institution (IFI) Framework for a Harmonized Approach to Greenhouse Gas Accounting. Baseline assumed as zero since the programme is new added generation.

outcomes.name: M6.0 Increased number of small, medium and large low-emission power suppliers
outcomes.description: South Africa has the 2nd largest economy in Sub-Saharan Africa, after Nigeria and according to World Economic Forum Global Competiveness Index. Eskom is South Africa’s primary electricity supplier and generates ca. 90% of the electricity used in South Africa, and approximately 40% of the electricity used on the African continent. In terms of climate change, most of South Africa’s electricity (ca 90%) is generated from coal-fired power stations. South Africa ranks amongst the top 20 energy intensive users in the world. The average CO2 intensity for the national grid (grid emission factor, EF) is estimated at 1.015 tCO2/ MWh10 for the South African grid.  In response to climate change, the REIPPPP that commenced in 2011 allowed the country to procure renewable capacity of up to 6,422 MW from 112 IPPs to date. There is uncertainty surrounding the continuance of the REIPPPP programme in its current form backed by a sovereign guarantees, in the present context of budgetary pressure on the government’s finances. Development and financing of RE projects without government guarantee/support will be challenging not only for banks, but also for sponsors  The DBSA, in line with the Paris Agreement commitments wishes to support the development of an alternate mechanism for direct power procurement by the consumers in a cost effective manner, while leveraging / crowding in private sector investors / developers and banks.   The proposed credit enhancement mechanism under the Programme will provide the banks with a comfort while allowing for the continuation of the addition of renewable energy programs through the addition of 330 MW of new generating capacity, generating approx. 744,600 MWh of clean electricity annually, thereby directly avoiding emissions of more than 717,794 tCO2e per annum.

4. Construction period differs for each window period (from 24 months to 40 months for wind projects) and (from  18 months to 28 months for Solar PV)
5. Operations jobs are accounted for over a 20 year period

The implementation of this Programme will have positive effects on climate change through reduction of GHGs and decrease of air emissions and other environmental pollutants. In addition, in a water- scarce country like South Africa, the projects will result in the reduction in water abstraction and consumption for electricity generation purposes

outcomes.name: Socio-economic through the inclusion of women
outcomes.description:
The Programme will highlight the importance of women inclusion in the sub-projects and thus create opportunities for employment and upskilling of women in the green sector. Gender inclusivity activities include the following: 
- Ensure increased participation of women owned enterprises in the Programme - Review eligibility/funding criteria of the programme to target participation of both men and women owned companies to ensure gender balance in benefits 
- Emphasize on the hiring of women at project level, for the implementation of project objectives - Review employment criteria to allow for gender sensitivity and inclusion of women.
-  Provide technical training and capacity building to improve long-term employability of women. Develop gender sensitive training programmes and capacity building programmes. The training programmes should be recognized by the relevant authorities

basic_info.title: Supporting Innovative Mechanisms for Industrial Energy Efficiency Financing in Indonesia with Lessons for Replication in other ASEAN Member States
basic_info.description:
Indonesia is in need of urgent actions to address gradually deteriorating climate change impacts and their consequences. The climate-vulnerable archipelagic state submitted its nationally determined contribution (NDC) to the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC) with a strong willingness to transition towards a low-carbon and climate-resilient pathway. The NDC of Indonesia recognises energy efficiency as one of its key mitigation actions. While Indonesia attributes 65.43% of the total greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions to the energy sector, the market penetration of energy efficient technologies and the ensuing energy savings have been long hampered by a range of barriers, such as access to finance, regulatory challenges, and a lack of capacity and awareness amongst relevant stakeholders. In addition,since the Coronavirus Disease (COVID-19) outbreak and spread, the state budget and relevant stimulus measures in the neediest areas have brought the agenda of energy efficiency and conservation back in terms of priority. Surrounded by further setbacks from the pandemic and related budget deficits, an international-level intervention in the urgent, but deprioritised area is more important than ever. The Programme entitled “Supporting Innovative Mechanisms for Industrial Energy Efficiency Financing in Indonesia with Lessons for Replication in the other ASEAN Member States,” hereafter “Programme,” will support Indonesia and its neighbouring ASEAN members to re-focus on climate actions amid the postpandemic recovery era, strengthening the foundation for the NDC achievement and the national alignment with the Paris Agreement eventually.
The Programme, therefore, shall support Indonesia and other ASEAN countries to be ready to drive a lowcarbon development pathway with enhanced energy efficiency and conservation performance, addressing three chronic barriers – i.e., financial, regulatory, and technical barriers, which are all interlinked with a vicious circle of implementation failures of similar precedents. This initiative pays special attention to the three challenges, regarding them as the three entry points of intervention and designing three components accordingly as below. The Programme proposes a solution package of innovative energy efficiency financing schemes equipped with de-risking mechanisms, a supportive regulatory framework, and technical assistance.

risk.description: A potential technical and operation risk might be caused by a lack of knowledge and competence of end beneficiaries – mainly industrial enterprise owners – surrounded by the least-developed energy efficiency market circumstances. Especially, industrial enterprises ought to adopt themselves to change into energy efficient equipment and technologies. The change necessarily requires enterprises to redefine the operation and management (O&M) process as per modification and/or replacement of technical methodology and operational mechanism.
risk.mitigation:
The eligibility criteria require local FIs and their borrowers (end beneficiaries) to carry out a technical assessment on underlying projects so that any potential technical and operational risk attributable to unfamiliarity and a lack of skill and knowledge could be minimised as much as possible.
The set criteria further require demonstration of a concrete O&M plan over the borrowing lifespan in order to ensure the energy efficiency system optimisation and energy saving results accordingly.
As per request from partner FIs, the Programme provides support for a technical assessment of the vulnerable group of local beneficiaries under Component 3.

risk.description: A potential governance risk might be caused by the Programme implementation governance of a standard guarantee intermediation structure under which KDB relies on local FIs with a full authority to select, appraise, structure, execute, lend, and manage sub-projects and their proponents. Given that KDB controls on local borrowers operate only via the FIs, the selected FIs are the key to implement the Programme faithfully in line with the eligibility criteria, for instance, including ESS and the gender lens investing principle. This would relate the classic principal-agent problem often arising due to inherently competing priorities between the principal (GCF and KDB) and the agent (local FIs).
risk.mitigation:
Partner FIs selection has been cautiously done with many rounds of discussions, including a firm institutional commitment to the GCF and KDB policies and guidelines.
In addition, a partial guarantee coverage arrangement (100% coverage not acceptable as per eligibility criteria) under Component 1 is a protective device to align FIs’ interest with that of the GCF and KDB, thereby minimising the chronic principal-agent issue.
On top of the above, the Programme puts multi-level contractual and institutional arrangements to be effective throughout all the business cycle in place: (1) the eligibility criteria for sub-project selection; (2) the Sub-Project Loan Agreement aligned with the FAA; and (3) the operation of the PIU.
Also, KDB will monitor FIs, through reporting and physical visits where possible, if FIs implement the Programme in line with the eligibility criteria, and the guidelines of KDB and GCF, which therefore expects periodic meetings between KDB and each of FIs, including the Guarantee Advisor.

risk.description: The GCF Guarantee Facility of Component 1 would be directly exposed to the risk by its nature, up to a preagreed guarantee coverage ratio. As typically seen in a credit guarantee or insurance product, the guarantor could be also exposed to any credit risk attributable to information-asymmetry.
risk.mitigation:
Shortlisting and selection of local FIs takes the credit rating and relevant reputation into account. In particular, SMI and IIF, under the current discussion, are reputable private financial institutions in Indonesia and their respective credit ratings are equivalent or close to sovereign rating of the country. As such, it does not foresee the credit risk to be potentially material.
As for the FI Own Loan to be backed by the GCF guarantee Cover, the FI would share any credit risk of the relevant borrowers up to the residual portion under the guaranteed FI Own Loan. With the said mechanism, any credit risk of the final beneficiary exposed to relevant FI is not insignificant, thus the moral hazard risk to be less likely to occur. 
What is more, equity injection from a final beneficiary could be told as another mitigation measure against any credit risk of borrowers.

risk.description: There is a F/X risk of the Indonesian Rupiah (IDR) against USD because the vast majority of FI Own Loan to final beneficiaries are likely to be provided in IDR as it is the case for a typical corporate loan to domestic borrowers; however, GCF funding (Guarantee) is provided only in USD. As the GCF Credit Guarantee is denominated and made available in USD, FIs (as guarantee recipients) may be exposed to a currency mismatch risk related to the currency of defaulted debt service amounts under the FI Own Loan (in IDR, but this currency mismatch risk is limited to the case where IDR appreciates against USD.
risk.mitigation:
The GCF Credit Guarantee will be designed in a way which the foregoing F/X risk would be partially absorbed by the GCF and F/X risk not absorbed by GCF is borne by local FIs.
F/X risk will be regularly monitored and managed by a professional Guarantee Advisor who would be employed by KDB.

risk.description: The programme entails multi-dimensional and multi-level legal and contractual arrangements with multiple parties, which might carry a potential legal risk in association with legality or validity and enforceability of any agreement or contract. Furthermore, dis-alignment amongst agreements and contracts governed by different jurisdictions might be another potential legal risk.
risk.mitigation:
In order to avoid a potential legal risk arisen by jurisdiction discrepancy, definitive agreements where KDB is a contracting party – i.e., FAA, Guarantee Agreement, and TA Service Agreement – are to be universally governed under English Law and potent disputes are to be resolved by arbitration administered by the Singapore International Arbitration Centre (SIAC) in accordance with the SIAC Arbitration Rules. This is to be incorporated in the agreements.
On the other hand, all the Sub-Project Loan Agreements to be entered between a FI and each borrower are likely to be governed by the Indonesian law – the host country law. That implies that all the sub-transactions are standard corporate loan financing, which are the core business areas of local FIs with an extensive track record over decades. In that regard, the occurrence of any legal risk, potentially caused by jurisdictional discrepancy seems marginal; and even if a potential risk occurs, local FIs are deemed capable enough to tackle the legal disputes by themselves as a contacting party versed in the local legal process.
In addition, all legal agreements in the programme will be carried out with support and advice from qualified independent legal advisors, which will minimize the legal issues and controversies.

risk.description: The GCF proceeds under the programme can also be utilised to support anti-terrorist activities, money laundering or prohibited activities policies by FIs or the final beneficiaries. Additionally, this risk of a failure to meet international standards with respect to preventing money laundering and/or financing of terrorism can present problems, such as obstacles to financial flows and/or implementation and/or payment processing.
risk.mitigation:
The programme under Component 1 is supporting credit guarantees with local FIs, who will provide FI Own Loans to the final beneficiaries. As FIs were or will be selected through the proper capacity assessment by KDB including AML/CFT (KYC) assessment, final beneficiaries must also undergo due diligence according to the Sub-Project Loan Agreement between the FI and the final beneficiary. Then, it is considered that such related risk has been substantially mitigated.
Also, Indonesia has the national regulatory system to monitor relevant issues of the financial institutions under the purview of central bank. Moreover, the potential Executing Entity under Component 1 has an internal regulation and policy about their investment activities, so the final beneficiaries’ activities under the programme will be properly screened and monitored.
Under Components 2 and 3, AML/CFT assessment on the Executing Entity (ACE) is already completed by KDB, and the activities of implementing entities will be monitored if they are in line with the ACE’s internal regulations agreed by KDB. Reporting of complaints and allegations of impropriety, wrongdoing etc., are
conducted by PIU (Programme Implementation Unit) for Business Coordination and Sustainability under KDB Supervision, which will be established in each local FI under Component 3.

risk.description: There should not be any United Nations Security Council resolutions imposing financial sanctions that would prohibit the engagement of GCF or the AE in the country or with individuals or entities under each Component of the programme. If there is any, any approvals, exemptions, licences, or other permissions are required.
risk.mitigation:
KDB has ilar measures announced by the United Nations (UN) or other international organisations (e.g., World Bank, OECD, IMF, etc.), and will not deal with the countries or companies included in the list. 
In addition, it was confirmed by the internal system that Indonesia and ACE are not included in the list as of now.

risk.description: There should not be any practice included in GCF and KDB prohibited practices lists, e.g., non-compliance with the E&S safeguards and gender discrimination risk as for employment, during the implementation and financing of the activities, under the GCF and KDB guidelines on prohibited practices.
risk.mitigation: KDB has an internal system that controls AML/CFT and other prohibited practices, including prohinitons against E&S and Gender mainstreaming standards, and all the activities under the Programme will be monitored and supervised by KDB if any of them is in the list, based on GCF and KDB guidelines.

risk.description: Regulatory barriers including lack of fiscal and non-fiscal incentives, fossil fuelled energy subsidies, etc. should be addressed for energy efficiency improvement in Indonesian industrial sector from a long term point of view. However, there is a risk that it will not improve to a satisfactory level, given that it needs to coordinate various opinions or interests between many kinds of government ministires and there may face some conflict with the economic development rationale which is one of the government’s main policy goals.
risk.mitigation:
The Programme is aiming to minimize this risk thorugh Activity 3.3 under Component 3, 
In addition, it has a structure that enables a successful programme implementation without a prompt improvement of policy and regulation through GCF guarantee Cover and de-risking scheme development. 
Also, if energy efficiency project is successfully implemented and its potential economic benefit is known to policy makers, the government’s willingness to improve the energy efficiency regulatory framework is expected to increase.

outcomes.description: – Energy Efficiency Finance; Backstopping credit risk of local FI’s own loan with GCF credit guarantee provision. Component 1 shall establish the guarantee facility that offers appropriate derisking mechanisms that are currently lacking in the market for successful energy efficiency trials of local FIs. The Programme provides GCF credit guarantee to LFIs (guarantee beneficiary) through KDB, backstopping credit risk of relevant financl beneficiaries as borrowers of loan to be provided by LFIs. Target Selection for Priority Industries and Energy Efficient Technologies. The Programme identifies five priority areas for energy efficiency financing implementation from the industrial sector, as a result of the Market Assessment. The industrial subsectors – (1) food and beverage, (2) textiles and apparel, (3) pulp and paper and wood, (4) chemical and petrochemicals, and (5) non-metallic minerals.

To avoid any confusion, this is not a financing component to provide any de-risking scheme to each project but a Technical Assistance (TA) component to introduce and develop de-risking mechanisms for better energy efficiency projects for the future in Indonesia.

outcomes.name: Generation of locally customised ESI/ESCO financing mechanisms
outcomes.description:
Technical Assistance (TA) for the Market Readiness. One of the major barriers for successful energy efficiency finance is the lack of experience and understanding about energy efficiency by major stakeholders, which can be classified by consumer – industry, supplier, and financial institutions, and external environment – regulatory framework, in terms of energy efficiency finance. In Component 3, successful energy finance with balanced roles of stakeholders is pursued through capacity building for each stakeholder, which will support the Indonesian market that is ready to accept advancements in energy efficiency, in line with the GCF standards in terms of environmental and gender risk management. To avoid any confusion, this is not a financing component to provide funding to any installation of new technology, etc., but a Technical Assistance (TA) component to build the capacity of local stakeholders including financial institutions, industry players, government authorities, and neighboring countries for better energy efficiency eco-system in Indonesia and ASEAN member states. 
Local FIs capacity building activities. This activity focuses on the establishment of reliable two-way communication channels to provide continuous support to local FIs, who are EE under Component 1. ACE, as the Executing Entity for Component 3, will organise regular committee meetings and workshops to exchange knowledge and lessons learnt regarding energy efficiency financing to support in their efforts in the market and provide open communication channels between the localised Korean banks’ subsidiaries and local financial institutions in Indonesia, with a support of KDB, as the Accredited Entity. In particular, the subsiciaries equipped with both local presence and global energy efficiency finance knowhow, through this committee, will shares advanced financial experiences and systems with the local banks, especially related to climate finance and ESG principles under ACE’s support, which means that the localised subsidiaries will act not only as the Executing Entities in Component 1, but also as the Implementing Entities (in-kind contribution) for Activity 3.1.
Activity 3.1 also plans to establish a programme implementation unit (PIU) in local FIs, primarily SMI and IIF, for the sake of practical support in terms of pipeline development, investment portfolio management, monitoring and evaluation for sustainability, and reporting to KDB on a regular basis to ensure success in the energy efficiency finance ecosystem. The PIU will be composed of GCF project managers, focal points, and consultants dispatched from external expert agencies or TA Providers under Activity 3.1. The Programme will require LFIs to establish a PIU where local Technical experts (incl. E&S and gender) are to be assigned and to provide advisory to support the development of pipeline, technical, E&S monitoring, evaluation & assessment activities of FIs, and even the developoent of pipeline. The anticipated pipeline will be in compliance with a set of eligibility criteria agreed upon by the GCF and KDB (Table 5), with a financing roadmap and time-bound action plan, and an operational capacity of implementing the Programme in line with sustainability principles, E&S, and gender safeguards.

outcomes.name: Spreading positive perceptions of EE Finance in Indonesia and ASAEN
outcomes.description: Sharing knowledge and understanding gained by the Programme with ASEAN member states. Regional replication of the Programme throughout ASEAN member states is intended for this activity, bearing in mind that not only Indonesia, but also the entire ASEAN region, has experienced tremendous increments in energy demand, and that the long-term energy supply outlook is likely less positive in the absence of energy efficiency and conservation measures. Component 3 shall collect, organise, disseminate, and display valuable lessons learnt, stimulating knowledge sharing actions for brainstorming and devising contextual fitness-based solutions for countries in needs. ACE, the Executing Entity of the component, has assessed existing energy efficiency and conservation financing schemes through the ASEAN region as below (see Table 9), which will be referred to as baseline with other considerations, when it comes to shortlisting candidate countries. In that most energy efficiency projects in this region are currently carried out through grants, including for Indonesia, lessons in energy efficiency guarantees experienced through this Programme can present a fresh financing perspective for other ASEAN countries in situations and circumstances similar to those of Indonesia.

outcomes.name: Economic benefits of industries overall with saved energy and jobs created accordingly
outcomes.description:
It expects to bring about economic co-benefits. The local job creation especially green jobs with income generation is the major economic benefit to the country, in addition to energy savings-caused profits in industries. The energy efficiency business plans the employment of professional and administrative human resources to install, manage, monitor, evaluate, and maintain the energy efficient appliance and equipment in factories and plants. It assumes considerable economic impacts given a pool of several hundreds of energy managers and 30 ESCOs currently registered across Indonesia. The emergence of green jobs created and sustained will relieve unemployment as much as scale up and replication of the Programme proceeds.
Transition towards an energy efficient economic structure will result in great economic productivity and longterm economic prosperity, making the country more competitive in the ASEAN economy. A recent study reveals that firms whose energy productivity – economic output per unit of energy use – is the worst in industry could further achieve growth in annual profits at a rate of 2.2% to 13.8% by bringing energy efficiency into the industrial processing line.21 Assuming such room for potential growth, the Programme is integral to endeavours to jumpstart Indonesia.

outcomes.name: Women’s active participation in the energy efficiency labour market, with an increase of skilled female engineers
outcomes.description: Very well aware of tremendous merits of women-empowered advancement in line with the energy efficient economy booming, the Programme submits gender assessment and action plan in both Programme and project levels. Since the inception stage, KDB gender and social development specialist therefore has engaged the concept development in pursuit of a holistic gender mainstreaming. Taking into account the two-step financing structure of the Programme, as the Accredited Entity, KDB emphasises the incorporation of a women-led SME framework and women’s empowerment through the technical and vocational training provision that seeks for higher skilled occupation of women on the energy efficiency labour market. Detailed activities are specified in G.2 (Gender assessment and action plan section). Gender co-benefits further relate to social co-benefits. Proactive women’s empowerment activities will turn out to produce positive externalities in diverse social areas, such as healthcare and education, referring to internationally proven cases that the skilled female workforce will gain confidence and voice in the household decision making process and their generated income could be invested in schooling of their children and nutritious food of their families. To put it succinctly, the positive externalities are to be extended beyond gender and relevant arenas.
